comment: R0 merge-interaction review of merge 03155d9 (fix/bug-audit-sweep x origin/main). 8 subsystem reviewers + adversarial verification. 7 confirmed (all survived refutation), 5 lows, 0 refuted.

confirmed.title: Incremental-skip (H2) can never be bypassed: `rescan` force flag is dead on macOS, so model installs/swaps never re-embed unchanged files (semantic search silently empty/stale)
confirmed.evidence: Ours' H2 added the unconditional unchanged-skip in insertOne: `let unchanged: Bool = { ... abs(a - b) < 0.000_001 ... }` (DBWriter.swift:331-343) and `if unchanged { return }` (DBWriter.swift:412), which returns BEFORE step 5 `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO clip_embeddings` (DBWriter.swift:480-485). Theirs added the wire-level force flag, but the merged runScan still stubs it: `// the macOS scan pipeline's incremental skip isn't wired to it yet (tracked in NEXT.md).  _ = rescan` (FileIDEngineMain.swift:385-388) — that comment was true on theirs (which had NO skip; insertOne always rewrote children, see `git show 4ff9789:.../DBWriter.swift`), but is false in the merged tree: the skip now exists and nothing can disable it. The Windows engine — the IPC contract reference — gates its incremental skip on the flag: `Honors a `rescan: true` IPC flag by passing an empty set here` (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/discovery.rs:142-144). clip_embeddings is written nowhere else (grep: only DBWriter.swift:482), and the app's CLIP install is user-initiated/skippable (WelcomeSheet.swift:40-50, FileIDApp.swift:64-71).
confirmed.trigger: (a) User dismisses the WelcomeSheet without downloading CLIP, scans a 50k-file library (rows written, clipEmbeddingBlob nil because MobileCLIPService isn't loaded — Tagging.swift:115). Later installs CLIP ViT-B/32 in Settings and rescans the same root: every file has prior failed=0 + identical size/mtime → unchanged=true → return at DBWriter.swift:412 → step 5 never executes → clip_embeddings stays empty forever. (b) Pre-merge DB carrying old-space MobileCLIP-S2 embeddings: first merged-build scan runs while the ViT-B/32 ONNX isn't installed → blob nil → old-space row survives (step 5 only writes `if let blob`); user installs ViT-B/32 and rescans → unchanged-skip → stale 512-d MobileCLIP vectors are permanently mixed with ViT-B/32 text-query vectors (CLIPTextEncoder.swift:10 itself warns this 'breaks search'). (c) Forward hazard: theirs' model-reset pattern (v12_face_model_reset wipes face_prints and relies on a rescan to repopulate) is blocked by the skip — today only masked by the accidental 2001→1970 epoch flip forcing one full reprocess; the next reset migration (e.g. the FaceAlign re-embed deferred in MACOS_LOCKSTEP_NOTES Part 3) will strand data with no recovery path.
confirmed.impact: Semantic search returns nothing (or garbage rankings from cross-space cosine) for every already-scanned file, silently and permanently — rescanning, the documented remedy, is a no-op. The only recovery is EngineClient.wipeAndRescan, which deletes the whole library DB and destroys person names and user tags. Also breaks cross-platform contract: the same `startScan(rescan:true)` command force-reprocesses on Windows but is ignored on macOS.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Thread the flag through: `runScan(... rescan:)` → `DBWriter(db:sink:coordinator:sessionID:forceReprocess: rescan)` and compute `let unchanged = !forceReprocess && { ... }` in insertOne. Additionally (cheap, fixes case (a) without a forced rescan): when `unchanged`, still run step 5 if `clipEmbeddingBlob != nil` and no clip_embeddings row exists (one EXISTS query). Longer term, mirror Windows' discovery-level skip_paths gating so unchanged files are skipped before tagging, not after.

verdict.reasoning:
I set out to refute this and could not — every link in the trigger chain checks out at HEAD (a3df5c3, which is 03155d9 + 3 commits that only add tests/corpus/release tooling; the cited code is byte-identical).

Mechanics verified at current HEAD:
1. The unconditional skip exists exactly as cited: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Storage/DBWriter.swift:331-343 computes `unchanged` from size/mtime/failed only; line 412 `if unchanged { return }` exits before step 5's `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO clip_embeddings` (lines 480-485). `insertOne` and `DBWriter.init` (line 112) have no force parameter.
2. The flag is dead twice over: FileIDEngineMain.swift:385-388 stubs it (`_ = rescan`), and the app never even sends true — EngineClient.swift:609 hardcodes `rescan: false`; the only other path is the destructive `wipeAndRescan` (EngineClient.swift:638-668, which deletes fileid.sqlite + WAL outright, destroying persons/user tags — the engine-side `.wipeLibrary` IPC is itself `not_implemented_yet` on macOS, FileIDEngineMain.swift:360-365).
3. No alternate writer or backfill: `clip_embeddings` is written only at DBWriter.swift:482 (Restructure.swift and ReadStore only SELECT); `MobileCLIPService.embedImage` is called only from Tagging.swift:115 and preWarm. macOS Discovery.swift has no incremental pre-filter, so the skip is purely write-side.

Trigger (a) survives: MobileCLIPService.swift:63-67 returns nil when the ONNX is missing ("callers degrade silently") and Tagging.swift does NOT set `failed` for a nil blob (failed only on decode error, line 83), so the first CLIP-less scan writes failed=0 rows with correct size/mtime. WelcomeSheet is a dismissible sheet with optional per-model install buttons; the Settings CLIPSemanticSearchCard install has no post-install rescan hook. A later rescan recomputes the embedding in Tagging (model now loads lazily from disk) and then discards it at DBWriter.swift:412. Permanent, silent.

Trigger (b) survives: ReadStore.rankByCosine (ReadStore.swift:242-268) reads ALL clip_embeddings rows with no `model` filter, guarding only on dimension — and both MobileCLIP-S2 and ViT-B/32 are 512-d, so stale old-space vectors rank against new-space text queries. Step 5 only writes `if let blob`, never deletes, so a CLIP-less reprocess leaves old rows intact. (Aggravating detail: DBWriter.swift:484 still labels new ViT-B/32 vectors "mobileclip_s2", so even a future model-column cleanup can't distinguish them.)

Trigger (c) survives: v12_face_model_reset (Database.swift:332-336) wipes face data and relies on rescan re-embedding; the claimed 2001→1970 epoch flip is real (550cd19's DBWriter used timeIntervalSinceReferenceDate, commit 1ad250b flipped to timeIntervalSince1970), confirming it's a one-time accidental mask — the next reset-style migration has no force-reprocess escape hatch.

Merge-interaction shape confirmed via both parents of 03155d9: ours (5e5289c) added the unconditional skip with no wire flag; theirs (4ff9789) added `rescan: Bool` + the `_ = rescan` stub while its insertOne always rewrote children (the stub was harmless there). The merge composed them into "skip always on, flag dead". The contract asymmetry is real: Windows honors rescan at platforms/windows/src/engine/src/scan_session.rs:161-163 (empty skip set on rescan=true).

Kill-attempts that failed: no failed-flag marking on missing CLIP; no backfill pass (faces have Stage D lazy extraction, CLIP has nothing); no post-install rescan trigger; no discovery pre-filter; no model-column filter in search; no migration wiping/refreshing clip_embeddings; the new DBWriterUpsertTests (2c2c269) pins only id/face/tag/FTS preservation — it never touches clip_embeddings or the rescan flag, so no test pin contradicts the scenario; NEXT.md, despite the code comment "(tracked in NEXT.md)", contains no item for wiring the macOS rescan flag (grep for rescan/incremental/wire/force finds only H2 semantics notes and Windows items).

Severity: keeping high. It silently and permanently breaks semantic search (a headline feature) for the mainstream skip-install-then-install-later flow, with the only recovery being whole-library destruction of user-entered data, plus a cross-platform IPC contract violation. Not critical because no existing data is corrupted or lost by the bug itself and the app is pre-release. The suggested fix (thread `rescan` into DBWriter as forceReprocess, plus the cheap EXISTS-based step-5 backfill when unchanged) is consistent with the verified code structure.

confirmed.title: Failed-file rescan now destroys valid face rows (incl. manual person assignments) and OCR text — merged kept ours' unconditional child-row DELETE, reverting the guarded-delete contract main audited on Windows (faces_evaluated / DECISIONS #5)
confirmed.evidence: Merged insertOne: a failed file is never 'unchanged' (`guard oldFailed == 0, !file.failed else { return false }`, DBWriter.swift:334), so it falls through to `DELETE FROM ocr_text WHERE file_id = ?` (DBWriter.swift:436) and `DELETE FROM face_prints WHERE file_id = ?` (DBWriter.swift:454-456) with empty replacement data (Tagging.swift:75-86 returns failed=true with empty visionTags/facePrints/ocr when loadCGImage fails). Theirs' parent preserved both on failure: faces were deleted only inside `if !bboxes.isEmpty` and ocr_text only REPLACE'd when new text existed (git show 4ff9789:.../DBWriter.swift, step 3/4). Main's line had explicitly audited this exact class on Windows: the stale-face DELETE is keyed 'on whether the face stage actually ran this session, NOT on faces.is_empty(): ... a face-disabled / GPU-dead session leaves still-valid rows intact (#5)' (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/dbwriter.rs:366-373) and DECISIONS.md:2792 ('The naive "always delete" would wipe valid faces on a models-missing rescan'). The merged macOS writer has no faces_evaluated equivalent. This also contradicts H2's own stated goal of preserving 'the user's MANUAL person assignments' (DBWriter.swift:319-322).
confirmed.trigger: Photo P on a NAS/external volume was scanned successfully (faces detected, user manually assigned them to a named person). User rescans; this time loadCGImage returns nil for P (SMB hiccup, file locked by another app, partial sync write) → TaggedFile{failed:true, empty children}. insertOne: oldFailed==0 but file.failed → unchanged=false → P's face_prints rows (person_id assignments + SFace embeddings), ocr_text row, and auto tags are deleted in the same transaction; nothing is re-inserted.
confirmed.impact: Transient read failures during any rescan permanently destroy derived data and the user's manual person assignments for those files: the photos vanish from the named person in the People tab and from OCR search. A later successful rescan re-detects faces but as fresh unassigned rows (person_id NULL) — anchors may re-infer cluster names, but the explicit per-face assignment is gone. Silent (only a JSONL warn).
confirmed.suggestedFix: In insertOne, when `file.failed` and a prior row exists, update only the files row (failed/error_message/scanned_at) and skip steps 2-4 entirely so prior tags/OCR/faces survive a transient failure — mirroring Windows' faces_evaluated semantics. Keep the unconditional deletes for genuinely changed, successfully-processed files (that part is the correct L2/L4 staleness fix).

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace confirms every link in the trigger narrative with no killing guards. (1) Tagging.swift:76-87 returns TaggedFile{failed:true} with default-empty visionTags/facePrints/faceBBoxes/ocrText when loadCGImage fails (SMB hiccup, lock, partial sync). (2) Discovery has no DB-backed incremental skip, so every rescan re-processes every file. (3) DBWriter.commit (lines 166-167) passes failed files to insertOne unfiltered. (4) The unchanged early-return at DBWriter.swift:334 ('guard oldFailed == 0, !file.failed else { return false }') guarantees a previously-successful, now-failed file falls through to the child-row writes. (5) It then executes DELETE FROM tags ... source='auto' (418-420), DELETE FROM ocr_text (436, v14 triggers strip FTS postings), and DELETE FROM face_prints (454-456 — deliberately hoisted outside the !bboxes.isEmpty guard per its own comment) with nothing re-inserted, inside the committed batch transaction; it also nulls phash. face_prints.person_id is where manual person assignments live (Database.swift:128; assignment writes are UPDATE face_prints SET person_id). (6) Merge regression verified: parent 4ff9789 (origin/main) deleted faces only inside `if !bboxes.isEmpty` and only REPLACE'd ocr_text when new text existed — both survived a failed read; merge 03155d9 kept ours' (5a9a197) unconditional deletes. (7) Windows contract verified: dbwriter.rs:366-373 keys the face delete on faces_evaluated, and tagging.rs:1466/1740-1746 sets it only in the successful-decode block, explicitly to avoid this exact bug; merged macOS has no equivalent. (8) The new H2 regression test (DBWriterUpsertTests.swift, commit 2c2c269) never exercises failed:true, so nothing pins this. Minor corrections: auto-tag loss on failure also existed in theirs (not a merge regression — faces/OCR are), and clip_embeddings survive (no delete in step 5). Severity stays medium: silent, irreversible loss of manual per-face person assignments and a broken People/OCR-search state until the next successful rescan, but scoped to files that transiently fail during a rescan, with tags/OCR/faces self-healing on a later successful scan and v13 anchors partially re-attaching clusters.
verdict.experiment: Not required — the path is statically deterministic. To pin it in CI: add a third drain to /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/Tests/EngineTests/DBWriterUpsertTests.swift sending makeFile(...) with failed=true, errorMessage set, and empty tags/ocr/faces for the already-assigned file, then assert face_prints count==1 with person_id intact and ocr_fts MATCH 'alpha' still returns the row. This test fails against current HEAD (run on the Mac via swift test).

confirmed.title: Range downloader stall tolerance collapsed: Line A's bounded 4-attempt budget composed with Line B's halved 60s read_timeout — each parent shipped a 'slow flaky connection must not fail the install' fix, the merge un-ships both
confirmed.evidence: Merged tree: line 113 `.read_timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))` (Line B, lowered from base/Line A's 120s) + line 823 `continue 'retry;` on mid-stream chunk error (Line A's H13) inside the fixed-size loop at 725-728 (`'retry: for (attempt, backoff) in std::iter::once(Duration::ZERO).chain(backoffs.iter().copied())` — exactly 4 iterations, backoffs [1s,4s,16s]), terminating at line 834 `anyhow::bail!("range exhausted retries (start={start})")`. Parents: Line B tip (03155d9^2) line 803 retried mid-stream errors via `return Box::pin(download_range_with_retry(...)).await` — a tail-recursive restart that granted a FRESH 4-attempt budget on every stall, i.e. unlimited stall recovery (its 60s timeout rationale at merged lines 63-70 explicitly says 'a genuinely stalled stream errors → resumes via download_range_with_retry within ~61 s ... BEFORE it alarms the user mid-recovery' — stalls are expected and routine). Line A tip (03155d9^1) had `read_timeout(120)` — H13's 'eventually bails instead of spinning forever' bound was calibrated against stall-errors being half as frequent. The merge keeps B's 'stall errors are routine, ~61s recovery' timeout policy and A's 'stream errors are budgeted, 4 max' retry policy simultaneously. The same budget is also consumed by 429/5xx (line 776-784), transport errors (line 770-773), and 200-on-resume part discards (line 793-800), so mixed flakiness compounds toward the 4-attempt cap.
confirmed.trigger: User on congested/spotty wifi installs a large model (e.g. Qwen2.5-VL 7B, 4.7 GB GGUF: 12 ranges of ~390 MB each). During one range's lifetime the stream goes silent >60s four times (or e.g. 2 stalls + one HF 429 + one connection reset). Each stall trips reqwest's 60s read_timeout → chunk Err → `continue 'retry` consumes one attempt → after the 4th the range bails 'range exhausted retries', failing the whole prewarm. On pre-merge Line B the identical network behavior recovered indefinitely (recursion = fresh budget, progress preserved each round); on pre-merge Line A only half the stalls occurred (120s timeout), making exhaustion far less likely.
confirmed.impact: The Welcome-sheet install on a slow/flaky connection — the exact scenario both lines independently shipped fixes for (B's read_timeout comment, A's H13 'resumes via Range') — now surfaces a red 'Couldn't download … check your connection and click Retry' (prewarm.rs model_download_failed) after as little as ~4 minutes of intermittent stalling. No data loss (parts resume on Retry, each Retry refreshes the budget), but the user must babysit a multi-GB download with repeated manual Retry clicks where the pre-merge engine rode it out unattended.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make the retry budget progress-aware in download_range_with_retry: record the part file's size at the top of each attempt; on the mid-stream-error `continue 'retry` (and at the next attempt's stat), if existing_len grew since the last attempt, reset the attempt counter (e.g. switch the for-loop to a `loop` with an explicit `attempts_without_progress` counter capped at 4). This preserves H13's two guarantees — no permit-holding recursion deadlock and no infinite zero-progress spin — while restoring Line B's tolerance of long flaky downloads. Apply the same pattern to download_simple's `chunk_err → continue 'attempts` for symmetry.

verdict.reasoning: Independently traced every cited line in the merge commit 03155d9 and both parents; all factual claims hold. Merged downloader.rs (unchanged through current HEAD 2c2c269): line 113 read_timeout=60s (Line B's value); lines 719-728 a fixed 4-iteration 'retry for-loop (once(ZERO)+[1s,4s,16s]); line 823 mid-stream chunk Err -> `continue 'retry` (Line A's H13 fix); line 834 bails "range exhausted retries". Transport errors (~772), 429/5xx (~784), and 200-on-resume discards (~800) are bare `continue`s consuming the same 4-attempt budget. Parent ^1 (Line A) had read_timeout=120s (line 70) with the same bounded loop; parent ^2 (Line B) had read_timeout=60s plus `return Box::pin(download_range_with_retry(...)).await` at line 803, which resets attempt=0 and re-stats the part file — unlimited stall recovery with progress preserved. B's doc comment promising routine ~61s stall recovery survives verbatim in the merged file (lines 63-70) while the mechanism it relies on is now capped at 3 resumes per range — the merged file contradicts itself. Downstream chain verified: download_parallel propagates the first range error (lines 572-581); prewarm.rs:341-384 emits model_download_failed ("check your connection and click Retry"); ModelInstallerService.cs:875 routes it to a Failed slot state — no auto-retry anywhere, Retry is manual. PARALLEL_PARTS=12 confirms ~390MB ranges for a 4.7GB GGUF. The composed behavior is strictly less stall-tolerant than BOTH parents: vs B, bounded vs unbounded recovery; vs A, the same budget drained by errors that fire at 60s instead of 120s (any 60-120s stall now consumes budget that A absorbed). No guard, cfg gate, or test pins this — mod tests only covers check_size_plausible. The only non-static element is the premise that flaky wifi yields 4 stall/throttle events within one range's lifetime, which is the finding's stated scenario, not an uncertain mechanism; the code path from that premise to the red Failed pill is fully statically traced. The suggested progress-aware-budget fix is sound: it preserves H13's no-permit-recursion and no-zero-progress-spin guarantees while restoring B's tolerance.
verdict.experiment: Not required for the verdict, but a deterministic regression test would be: spin up a local hyper/axum test server that serves Range requests and injects 4 read-stalls longer than read_timeout (or 4 mid-body connection resets) across one range while bytes accumulate between stalls; assert pre-merge-B behavior (completion) vs merged behavior (bail "range exhausted retries") and that a progress-aware budget completes.

confirmed.title: Composite content-hash recipe drift silently kills rename-heal for >16 MB files in DBs written by origin/main builds
confirmed.evidence: Merged hash_with_threshold adds interior samples to the composite: `const INTERIOR_SAMPLES: u64 = 4; const INTERIOR_CHUNK: usize = 64 * 1024; for k in 1..=INTERIOR_SAMPLES { let off = size.saturating_mul(k) / (INTERIOR_SAMPLES + 1); ... hasher.update(&mid[..n]); }` (content_hash.rs:50-62). `git show origin/main:platforms/windows/src/engine/src/util/content_hash.rs` has NO such block — main's recipe is blake3(head 1MB ‖ tail 1MB ‖ size_le), and main's tagging.rs >16MB image path computed the byte-identical in-memory composite (`hasher.update(&bytes[..span]); hasher.update(&bytes[start_tail..]); hasher.update(&(bytes.len() as u64).to_le_bytes())`). For any size > 16 MB the merged digest provably includes 4 interior 64 KB chunks (off=size*k/5 always clears the 1 MB head/tail spans), so old and new digests differ for the SAME bytes. The heal lookup matches `content_hash IS NOT NULL AND content_hash = ?2` exactly (dbwriter.rs:679-681); there is no recipe-version column, no migration touching content_hash (db/migrations.rs v8 comment at 316-317 still describes the OLD 'head+tail+size' recipe), and no orphan-pruner for files rows (grep: only bulk.rs:382 deletes by explicit id). The merged build computes hashes only via the new recipe (tagging.rs:952-953 for over-cap images, tagging.rs:1238-1240 fallback for >16MB videos/docs/audio).
confirmed.trigger: User has scanned with any origin/main build (the on-hardware-verified production line) — every >16 MB file row (all videos, RAW/TIFF, large JPEGs) stores an old-recipe composite. They upgrade to the merged build, then move or rename such a file in a way where file_ref does not match: cross-volume move, NAS/SMB share (e.g. the G:\TrueNAS corpus) where the volume file ID is absent/unstable, or copy-then-delete tools. Next scan: the decoder/worker computes the NEW-recipe composite for the file at its new path; HEAL_LOOKUP_SQL finds no row with that hash (the stored one is old-recipe), heal_candidate_moved is never reached, and the INSERT creates a brand-new row.
confirmed.impact: Silent loss of catalog continuity for the entire >16 MB file class after upgrade: the moved file's tags, CLIP embeddings, faces, OCR/doc text do not follow it (fresh row, full recompute), and the old row remains forever at a dead path (no files-row orphan pruning exists) — the Library shows a ghost duplicate. Exactly the failure rename-heal was built to prevent; invisible to the 251 passing tests because they only pin the new recipe (content_hash.rs tests, dbwriter B1 tests).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pick one before shipping the merge: (a) during heal lookup, when size > FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES also compute the legacy head+tail+size digest and match `content_hash IN (?new, ?legacy)` (cheap: head+tail already read), upgrading the stored hash to the new recipe via the existing `content_hash = COALESCE(excluded.content_hash, ...)` upsert; or (b) add a vN+1 migration that NULLs content_hash WHERE size_bytes > 16777216 AND forces those rows' rescan (failed-style retry) so hashes are re-stamped under the new recipe before any move happens; also update the stale recipe description at db/migrations.rs:316-317. Option (a) is the only one that heals moves that happen before the re-stamp.

verdict.reasoning: Every element of the trigger narrative survives independent trace. (1) Recipe drift: git show origin/main:.../util/content_hash.rs has no interior-sample block; HEAD's hash_with_threshold (content_hash.rs:50-62) adds 4x64KB interior chunks whose skip-guard (off=size*k/5 vs 1MB span) admits all 4 samples for any size > ~5.6MB — so for every file above FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES (16MiB) the new digest hashes 256KB of extra input and provably differs from the old digest for identical bytes. The change landed on this branch in ad3184a (hash-collision fixes L30/M25/L26); merge-base 4ff9789 and origin/main both have the old recipe, and merge 03155d9 carries the new one with no compat path. (2) Main-built DBs contain old-recipe hashes, not NULLs: main's tagging.rs over-cap image branch computed an in-memory composite byte-identical to old hash_with_threshold (head span / start_tail / len.to_le_bytes), and its worker fallback called old content_hash() for >16MB video/doc/audio. (3) No mitigation exists: HEAL_LOOKUP_SQL (dbwriter.rs:673-684) is exact equality on content_hash or file_ref; no legacy-digest fallback anywhere; incremental scans skip unchanged files via a path-keyed skip set (scan_session.rs:161-236, scanned_at >= modified_at) so old rows are never re-stamped; migrations v10-v14 never touch content_hash or scanned_at (v12 wipes only the face graph); the v8 comment at migrations.rs:316-317 still documents the old recipe. (4) file_ref does not rescue the cited cases: platform.rs:544 returns the volume-local NTFS file index, which changes on cross-volume move/copy-then-delete and is None on open failure, leaving content_hash as the sole heal key exactly where the finding says. (5) Ghost row: the only files-row DELETE is the user trash flow (bulk.rs:382); the dbwriter comment at 298-299 referencing 'orphan-pruning' describes machinery that does not exist. (6) Tests are blind: content_hash tests pin only current-recipe determinism; dbwriter heal tests use synthetic hash bytes. Severity adjusted from high to medium on deployment reality, not mechanics: the Windows app is pre-release (unsigned installer; SHIP.md is a readiness inventory), macOS never populates content_hash (apple DBWriter.swift:354 'content_hash/file_ref aren't computed by the macOS scan path yet') so there is no cross-platform DB exposure, and v1.0 fresh installs are recipe-consistent — the at-risk population is internal/dev DBs (the G:\TrueNAS-verified library), with a crude workaround (forced full rescan re-stamps hashes via the COALESCE upsert before any moves). It escalates back to high if any pre-merge build's DB exists outside the dev machines, so suggested fix (a) — match content_hash IN (new, legacy) for size > FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES and let the upsert re-stamp — should still land before ship; the stale migrations.rs:316-317 recipe comment and the false 'orphan-pruning' comment at dbwriter.rs:298-299 should be corrected with it.
verdict.experiment: Not needed for the verdict (fully established statically), but a pinning regression test would be: in dbwriter tests, seed a files row whose content_hash is the LEGACY digest of a >16MiB fixture (head1MB||tail1MB||size_le, computable in-test), delete the fixture's old path, then ingest the same bytes at a new path with file_ref=None and assert ingest_with_heal re-binds the existing row id — it currently inserts a fresh row, demonstrating the drift.

confirmed.title: Over-cap (>16 MB) images lose EXIF camera/GPS — ours' OOM fix relies on a worker EXIF fallback that theirs had deleted
confirmed.line: 943-954 (over-cap branch, exif_data left None), 1352-1361 (worker consumes Option with no fallback), 920-925 (stale comment)
confirmed.evidence: Merged over-cap branch: `FileKind::Image => { // Over-cap image ... EXIF falls back to the worker's bounded parse. content_hash = crate::util::content_hash::content_hash(&file.path, file.size_bytes).ok(); }` — it never sets `exif_data`, and `file_bytes` stays None. The worker consumption is `if let Some((cam, lat, lon)) = exif { tagged.camera_model = cam; ... }` (tagging.rs:1354-1358) with NO parse fallback: the only `exif::Reader::new().read_from_container` call in the entire crate is the ≤16 MB decoder branch (tagging.rs:928, verified by grep). The ≤16 MB branch's own comment (920-925) records why: theirs removed `parse_exif_blocking` as unreachable because main's decoder pre-read EVERY image (`git show origin/main:...tagging.rs`: single `FileKind::Image` arm, read_to_end + in-memory EXIF for all sizes). Ours' H6/ENG-71 OOM fix (commit 78d569c) reintroduced a no-pre-read path without restoring the fallback its comment promises; the merged tree keeps that combination.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a library containing decodable images larger than 16 MB with EXIF — e.g. 20-30 MB JPEGs from 40-45 MP cameras, multi-frame TIFF scans, drone panoramas (under the 50 MP MAX_DECODED_PIXELS cap so they decode fine). On origin/main these rows persisted camera_model + location_lat/lon; on the merged build `exif` arrives as None and the row is written with camera_model = NULL, location = NULL.
confirmed.impact: Silent metadata regression vs the verified main line for exactly the high-value photos: camera-family tag chips (push_enriched_extras, tagging.rs:1844-1859) disappear; restructure planning loses the Places bucket for these files (classify uses location_lat/lon at pipeline/restructure.rs:192) and EXIF-driven grouping; should_run_ocr (tagging.rs:1768-1770) no longer skips camera photos, wasting OCR CPU and risking junk has_text rows. No test covers EXIF for over-cap images, so the suite cannot see it.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In the over-cap FileKind::Image arm, do a bounded head read (the EXIF APP1/TIFF IFD lives in the first bytes; reading 1-4 MB is enough and matches the composite-hash head read) and run the same exif::Reader parse on that buffer to populate exif_data; alternatively restore a worker-side bounded parse and make the comment true. Either way add a regression test that a >FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES JPEG fixture still yields camera_model/GPS.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at HEAD of fix/bug-audit-sweep (merge 03155d9 is in this branch's history; current tip 2c2c269 carries the same code). Independent trace of /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/tagging.rs:

1) Over-cap arm (lines 943-954): `FileKind::Image =>` (size > FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES = 16 MB, content_hash.rs:17) sets only `content_hash` via the composite hash; `exif_data` and `file_bytes` both stay None. Its comment "EXIF falls back to the worker's bounded parse" (line 951) is false.

2) The fallback does not exist. Grep over the whole crate finds exactly one `exif::Reader::new().read_from_container` call — line 928, inside the ≤16 MB arm. `parse_exif_blocking` appears only in two stale comments (lines 922, and the same in main). `git log -S parse_exif_blocking` shows it was deleted in 9e364f7 ("single-read finalization"), which is an ancestor of BOTH merge parents — so neither side had a worker fallback at merge time.

3) Worker consumption (lines 1352-1361): `if let Some((cam, lat, lon)) = exif { ... }` with no else/parse path; `exif` comes solely from `PreDecoded.exif` (destructured at line 1190, built at line 1023). No other code assigns camera_model/location_lat/lon anywhere in the engine (dbwriter only persists `excluded.camera_model`; restructure.rs:388 is a test fixture).

4) The trigger is reachable: discovery has no size filter; `decode_image_sync(path, None)` (lines 1086-1152) mmaps the file and decodes any image under MAX_DECODED_PIXELS = 50,000,000 (line 1889). A 20-30 MB, 40-45 MP JPEG decodes fine, `image_source` is Some, the kind==Image block at 1352 runs, `exif` is None, and the row is written with camera_model/location NULL.

5) Regression vs origin/main verified: `git show 03155d9^2` (= origin/main at merge) has a single `FileKind::Image` arm with unconditional `read_to_end` + in-memory EXIF parse for ALL sizes (its lines 906-924), so over-cap images got camera/GPS there. The split arms came from branch commit 78d569c (H6 OOM fix), and the merge kept them, so the reconciled line regresses.

6) No test pins this: the only integration test is discovery_throughput.rs; no EXIF or over-cap fixture exists anywhere under the engine tests.

One correction to the finding's framing and impact: (a) this is less a merge interaction than a branch-side regression introduced by 78d569c that the merge faithfully preserved — `parse_exif_blocking` was deleted in shared history (9e364f7), not uniquely by "theirs"; the merged behavior is identical to ours pre-merge (03155d9^1 lines 715-726). The regression-vs-main claim still holds because main was the verified baseline. (b) The should_run_ocr impact sub-claim is overstated: that function's default fallthrough returns false (skip OCR), so losing camera_model only flips the OCR decision for over-cap camera photos whose filenames contain scan/receipt/invoice/document/screenshot or that are PNGs — typical DSC_*.jpg camera files still skip OCR. The chip loss (push_enriched_extras, lines 1844-1859) and Places-bucket loss (restructure.rs ~193-199 `if let (Some(lat), Some(lon)) = (f.location_lat, f.location_lon)`) are real.

Severity stays medium: silent, test-invisible metadata loss confined to >16 MB decodable images with EXIF (high-MP JPEGs, large TIFFs, drone panoramas) — exactly the high-value subset — but no crash, no corruption, and recoverable by rescan once fixed (rows aren't marked failed, so a forced reprocess or the suggested head-read fix restores the data).
verdict.experiment: Not required for the verdict, but the cheapest runtime proof: add a cargo test in the engine that writes a >16 MB JPEG fixture (e.g. a valid 45 MP JPEG with EXIF Model + GPS, padded under 50 MP), runs run_decoder_thread + the worker path, and asserts camera_model/location_lat are Some — it fails on HEAD and passes on origin/main.

confirmed.title: Per-row Cancel with null CurrentModelKind degrades to cancel-ALL / lost-cancel — Install All's pre-stamp window defeats line B's per-row-cancel contract
confirmed.evidence: WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:407 `_ = SafeRunAsync(() => Svc.CancelModelAsync(slot.CurrentModelKind), ...)` for any Downloading row. ModelInstallerService.cs:431 `if (modelKind is null) return EngineClient.Instance.CancelPrewarmAsync(null);` — the documented contract two lines above says per-row cancel exists "so cancelling one row no longer aborts every other concurrent install" (426-428). But InstallAllAsync pre-stamps every slot to Downloading (363 `slot.Status = ModelInstallStatus.Downloading`) after `slot.ResetForRetry()` (362), which NULLS CurrentModelKind (ModelSlot.cs:216); CurrentModelKind is only set at PrewarmAsync line 647, AFTER `WaitForReadyAsync(WaitForReadyTimeout)` (617) — up to 75 s on a cold-starting engine. Engine side: prewarm.rs:76-93 `cancel_prewarm(None)` sets EVERY flag in PREWARM_CANCELS; app side CancelPrewarmAsync(null) (EngineClient.Commands.cs:626) sets `_prewarmCancelAllRequested=1` killing all per-kind stall guards. During cold start SendCommandAsync also hard-faults (EngineClient.Commands.cs:46-51 'Engine not ready') and each later PrewarmModelAsync resets the cancel flags (Commands.cs:514-516; engine reset_prewarm_cancel at dispatch), so the cancel is silently dropped.
confirmed.trigger: Canonical first-run flow: welcome sheet appears while the engine is still cold-starting; user clicks 'Install all' → all five rows pre-stamp to Downloading ('Queued — starting download…') with CurrentModelKind=null for up to 75 s; user clicks Cancel on the 6.1 GB Qwen row → CancelModelAsync(null) → cancel-all that (a) is entirely lost on cold start (engine flag map empty / flags reset at dispatch, send may even fault on not-Ready), so the row shows 'Cancelling…' then starts downloading anyway; or (b) if the engine is Ready and other prewarms are already in flight (e.g. LlamaRuntimeAutoInstaller's runtime pack, or the click lands in the pre-stamp→kind-set gap), kills row Y's and the auto-installers' downloads too and disables every per-kind stall guard via _prewarmCancelAllRequested.
confirmed.impact: User-visible broken cancel in the most common onboarding path: cancelling one multi-GB model either does nothing (download proceeds after engine-ready despite 'Cancelling…') or aborts unrelated concurrent downloads — exactly the cross-row abort B's per-row cancel was written to prevent. (Provenance: the CancelModelAsync machinery is line B's; line A's per-row cancel was deliberately CancelAllAsync. The merge adopted B's contract, so the null-kind hole is now the shipped behavior; it was not present at the merge base.)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make the null case a no-op-with-log (or pass the slot, not the kind): in WelcomeSheet.HandleAction pass the ModelSlot and have ModelInstallerService derive kinds from slot identity (it already owns SlotKindGroups + SentinelIdsFor); also set slot.CurrentModelKind during the InstallAllAsync pre-stamp (e.g. to the slot's primary kind) so the Downloading state is never kind-less. Never translate a per-row Cancel into CancelPrewarmAsync(null).

verdict.reasoning:
Attempted refutation failed — every link of the trigger chain verifies statically at HEAD (2c2c269; only an unrelated SankeyFlowControl.cs line changed since merge 03155d9).

CHAIN VERIFIED:
1. Pre-stamp window: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Services/ModelInstallerService.cs:359-366 — InstallAllAsync calls slot.ResetForRetry() (which nulls CurrentModelKind, ModelSlot.cs:216) then sets Status=Downloading. CurrentModelKind is set only at ModelInstallerService.cs:647, AFTER `await WaitForReadyAsync(WaitForReadyTimeout)` (line 617, timeout = 75 s, line 65). WaitForReadyAsync (EngineClient.cs:944-976) returns Task.CompletedTask when Ready, so the null-kind window equals the engine cold-start duration — the code's own comment (ModelInstallerService.cs:60-65) admits cold start legitimately exceeds 30 s, and the welcome sheet appears at Content.Loaded (MainWindow.xaml.cs:134-139), i.e. before engine Ready on first run.
2. Cancel button is live in that window: WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:190 labels a Downloading row "Cancel"; HandleAction:397-407 sets Message="Cancelling…" and calls Svc.CancelModelAsync(slot.CurrentModelKind) — null during the pre-stamp window.
3. Null degrades to cancel-all: ModelInstallerService.cs:431 `if (modelKind is null) return EngineClient.Instance.CancelPrewarmAsync(null);` — directly contradicting the per-row contract documented at lines 426-428.
4. Lost-cancel branch (cold start): EngineClient.Commands.cs:626 sets _prewarmCancelAllRequested=1, then SendCommandAsync faults at lines 46-51 (State != Ready) — the CancelPrewarmCommand never reaches the engine. WelcomeSheet.SafeRunAsync (lines 469-479) swallows the fault with only a DebugLog.Warn. Nothing flips the slot out of Downloading; when the engine reaches Ready, PrewarmAsync resumes (skips reset because Status==Downloading, line 640), sets the kind, and PrewarmModelAsync clears _prewarmCancelAllRequested (Commands.cs:511 — finding cited 514-516, off by a few lines, substance correct). Result: "Cancelling…" → "Queued — starting download…" → the 6.1 GB download proceeds. Cancel silently lost. (App-side IsPrewarmCancelled only gates the stall guard, never aborts a download, so the transient flag does nothing.)
5. Cancel-all crossfire branch (Ready engine): engine main.rs:527-530 dispatches cancel_prewarm(payload.model_kind.as_deref()); prewarm.rs:76-94 None-arm sets EVERY flag in PREWARM_CANCELS — aborting all in-flight downloads (incl. LlamaRuntimeAutoInstaller.cs:113 / CudaAutoInstaller.cs:141 auto-installs) — and _prewarmCancelAllRequested=1 disables every per-kind stall guard (IsPrewarmCancelled, Commands.cs:502-504, checked at 578/583).

One narrative element is weaker than stated: the "click lands in the pre-stamp→kind-set gap while engine is Ready" sub-branch of (b) is a milliseconds-wide race (WaitForReadyAsync short-circuits when Ready) and is implausible for a human click. But branch (b) does not depend on it — the null-kind Downloading state also arises for whole-download durations via paths that bypass ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync entirely: SettingsView.xaml.cs:893-894, DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:723, and CudaAutoInstaller.cs:141 call EngineClient.PrewarmModelAsync directly, and the progress router (HandleProgress → ModelSlot.Apply, ModelInstallerService.cs:847-865) flips slots to Downloading without ever setting CurrentModelKind (Apply never writes it). E.g. the Intel OpenVINO background auto-install (ort_openvino_x64 → Accelerator slot via SlotFor:747) puts a Cancel-able, kind-less Downloading row on the welcome sheet during onboarding; clicking it cancel-alls the user's own Install All.

PROVENANCE VERIFIED: at merge-base f8f2603 and parent1 (5e5289c, line A) the per-row Cancel was `Svc.CancelAllAsync()` (WelcomeSheet:380) — intentional, documented cancel-everything; parent2 (4ff9789, origin/main, line B) introduced `CancelModelAsync(slot.CurrentModelKind)`; merge 03155d9 adopted B. So the lost-cancel half did not exist pre-merge, and the cross-row abort was pre-merge intended behavior rather than a contract violation — the finding's merge-interaction framing is accurate.

NO KILLERS FOUND: no guard nulls-out the Downloading+Cancel combination during pre-stamp; SafeRunAsync swallows the send fault; no retry/queue of the cancel; engine never sees it on cold start; per-kind flag resets at PrewarmModel dispatch (main.rs:519) and app-side flag clear (Commands.cs:511) erase any residue. The only timing assumption — that engine cold start lasts long enough for a user to click Install All then Cancel — is attested by the codebase itself (30→75 s timeout rationale) and requires no hardware to accept.

Severity stays medium: user-visible broken cancel in the canonical onboarding path (lost cancel of a 6.1 GB download, or aborting unrelated concurrent installs + disabling all stall guards), but recoverable (a second Cancel click after the kind is stamped works, per-kind path at line 433-434) and no data loss or crash.
verdict.experiment: Not required for the verdict, but on-hardware confirmation: cold-boot first run, click "Install all" within ~5 s of the welcome sheet appearing, immediately Cancel the Qwen row, and observe app.log for "[IPC OUT] CancelPrewarm ABORTED — Engine not ready" followed by the qwen2_5_vl_7b download proceeding past "Cancelling…".

lows.title: vlm.rs caption(): merge kept both H8 resolutions — ours' stderr-drain task is dead code under theirs' Stdio::null, silently dropping the diagnostic capture line A intended and leaving contradictory comments
lows.evidence: Line 217: `cmd.stderr(std::process::Stdio::null());` (theirs' H8 fix, with comment 'Discard stderr rather than pipe-and-not-read it'). Immediately after spawn, lines 224-237 carry ours' H8 fix: comment 'Drain stderr concurrently... Read it to EOF on its own task.' followed by `if let Some(stderr) = child.stderr.take() { tokio::spawn(async move { ... tracing::debug!(target: "vlm", "{l}") ... }) }`. Because stderr is Stdio::null, `child.stderr` is always None and the drain task never spawns. `git diff 4ff9789 03155d9 -- .../vlm.rs` confirms the merge's only delta vs origin/main is the addition of this dead block; ours (5e5289c) had `cmd.stderr(std::process::Stdio::piped())` paired with the drain.
lows.trigger: Any Deep Analyze per-file caption through the CLI fallback (llama-mtmd-cli). The child writes tens of KB of load/backend/timing diagnostics to stderr; the OS discards them. The drain task — and its tracing target "vlm" debug log of llama.cpp stderr — never executes.
lows.impact: No hang (both fixes independently prevent the H8 deadlock), but line A's intent is silently lost: VLM stderr diagnostics (model/mmproj load errors, GPU backend init failures) never reach the engine log even at debug level, while the code and its comment claim they do. When a caption fails with 'VLM exited with status N' there is no stderr breadcrumb to diagnose it. The two adjacent contradictory comment blocks ('Discard stderr' vs 'Drain stderr... Read it to EOF') invite a future editor to rely on logs that cannot exist.
lows.suggestedFix: Pick one resolution: either (a) restore line A's behavior — change line 217 to `cmd.stderr(std::process::Stdio::piped())` and keep the drain task (preserves diagnostics, still deadlock-free), or (b) keep Stdio::null and delete the dead drain block at lines 224-237 plus its comment so the file states one consistent contract.

lows.title: Schema command generateVideoThumbnail has no Swift IPCCommand.Payload case — macOS cannot round-trip it and the cross-platform symmetry contract is broken
lows.evidence: ipc.schema.json:232 defines the command: '{ "type": "object", "required": ["generateVideoThumbnail"], ... }' with no Windows-only annotation. Rust has it (ipc/mod.rs:175-176 '#[serde(rename = "generateVideoThumbnail")] GenerateVideoThumbnail(GenerateVideoThumbnailPayload)' + main.rs:825 dispatch arm) and C# has it (CommandPayload.cs:144-146 'GenerateVideoThumbnailCommand', converter arms at 194 and 252). Swift's IPCCommand.Payload (IPCProtocol.swift:18-83) has every other schema command — including the merge-incoming markPersonsDifferent (line 75) and wipeLibrary (line 79) — but NO generateVideoThumbnail case, even though the matching reply event IS mirrored ('case thumbnailGenerated(ThumbnailGenerated)' at line 175 and the ThumbnailGenerated DTO at lines 762-772). The Swift comment at lines 52-57 states the contract these cases exist to satisfy: 'These land on mac only when the schema needs to round-trip them (cross-platform tooling, shared test corpus). The mac engine dispatcher returns a structured "not_implemented_yet" error for each'. FileIDEngineMain.swift:117-119 shows what happens instead for an unknown case: 'JSONLog.shared.warn(ev: "command_decode_failed", ...); await sink.emit(.error(EngineError(kind: "command_decode_failed", ...)))'.
lows.trigger: Any cross-platform tool, shared test-corpus harness, or future shared EngineClient feeds the macOS engine an IPCCommand line '{"id":"...","payload":{"generateVideoThumbnail":{"path":"/Users/x/clip.mp4","modifiedAt":1700000000.0}}}' (the exact frame the Windows app emits). Swift's keyed decoder finds no matching CodingKey, LineReader yields .failure, and the engine emits a raw 'command_decode_failed' error instead of the structured 'not_implemented_yet' pointer every other Windows-originated command gets. Provenance: line B (origin/main) added the command to schema+Rust+C# without the Swift case; line A never had the command at all, so relative to this audit branch the gap is merge-incoming (it is NOT a hand-resolution artifact — the merged Swift file is byte-identical to origin/main's).
lows.impact: The schema's stated symmetry invariant ('The schema keeps these symmetric across platforms' — FileIDEngineMain.swift:343) no longer holds for exactly one command; cross-platform round-trip tooling decodes 29/30 commands and hard-fails on the 30th with an undifferentiated decode error, and the schema gives no signal that this is intentional (no 'Windows-only' marker, unlike the Swift-side doc comment verifyCudaPack gets at IPCProtocol.swift:80-81). No end-user impact today (no macOS sender; test corpus is file-based, not IPC-based).

Risk 1 (stale-exit swallow): CLEAN. The merged guard (EngineClient.cs:819-827) swallows an exit only when sender != _process. _process can be null at callback delivery only after the SAME process's own Cleanup (impossible — Exited fires once per Process and Cleanup unsubscribes at line 919) or after Dispose() (never called: grep shows no EngineClient.Instance.Dispose call site). The only reachable swallow is a replacement process already installed at line 561 (`_process = p`), in which case a live engine exists and not respawning/counting is correct. A real crash always has sender == _process, so Cleanup (line 829), the _expectingExit consume (line 842), the 3-strike terminal (lines 867-872, 'Engine crashed three times in a row') and the 1s/4s/16s respawn (lines 875-896) all still execute.

Risk 2 (mid-StartAsync interleave): no double-spawn reachable. If the old process's Exited callback fires during the `await Task.Run(WVT)` window (line 444) it Cleanups the OLD state and schedules a respawn, but that respawn either loses the strict _isStarting CAS (line 406, gate held for StartAsync's whole body via try/finally release at line 601) or hits the `_process is { HasExited: false }` early-return (line 393) once the original StartAsync installed the new process before releasing the gate. UI-thread-called StartAsync (app launch, wipe stage 4, respawn path) cannot be interleaved inside the no-await spawn block (lines 507-575). Residual cost is the phantom failure-count noted under preexisting (present identically on both parents).

Risk 3 (M15 after RestartAsync): intact. Normal restart: ShutdownAsync sets ticks+flag (Commands.cs:232-233); either the exit callback consumes the in-window flag (EngineClient.cs:842-849, State=Crashed/'stopped', no respawn — then RestartAsync's StartAsync respawns explicitly) or StartAsync's entry-clear (line 426) precedes it. A crash AFTER the restart completes has _expectingExit==0 (cleared at line 426 before any await) → counted and respawned. The M15 wedged-shutdown case (StopAndWait times out, engine alive, StartAsync early-returns at line 393 WITHOUT clearing the flag): a later real crash >60 s falls through the staleness check (lines 843-854, 'stale _expectingExit ignored') and respawns; <60 s is treated as the user's late-landing stop — by design.
